chosen_title: Apple 'Hide My Email' Vulnerability Reveals Peoples' Real Email Addresses
label: TECH
headline: Apple's Privacy Shield Is Leaking
image_prompt: A sleek, polished digital padlock suspended in a deep black void, its casing hairline-cracked down the center with a bright accent glow bleeding outward through the fracture like pressurized light escaping containment. Around the padlock, dozens of translucent ghost-layers — like peeled masks or stacked veils — drift apart and dissolve into the darkness, suggesting identities being slowly uncovered. The surface of the lock is cold brushed metal and frosted glass, pristine except for that single crack. The mood is quiet and unsettling, the kind of failure nobody noticed until it was too late. The lower third of the frame fades to pure shadow, calm and unlit.
tweets: Apple built 'Hide My Email' to make your real address invisible to every app on the internet. A newly discovered vulnerability does the exact opposite. 🧵

Hide My Email is an iCloud+ feature that replaces your real address with a randomly generated alias — apps see only the alias; Apple secretly forwards the mail. Your identity stays hidden. That's the entire promise of the product.

Security researchers found a flaw in how Apple handles the relay system. Someone with your alias and knowledge of the bug can potentially reverse it — exposing the real address you were trying to protect. Reported by 404 Media.

Who's most exposed:
• People who used aliases to sign up for untrusted services
• Anyone relying on it to avoid stalkers or harassment
• iCloud+ subscribers who paid specifically for this privacy layer

The brutal part: Hide My Email is exactly what you reach for when privacy matters most — sensitive signups, avoiding people who'd misuse your info. A leak there isn't just a bug. It's a failure at your last line of defense.

A privacy tool that exposes what it's built to protect is the worst-case vulnerability — because users trusted it precisely because Apple told them to.
Follow @decodededge
Source: 404 Media
